title: ANT/ENT Endings and -mente Adverbs
description: Words ending in -ANT or -ENT become Spanish by adding -E. Introduction to -mente adverbs.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Es. Good. We can use this with some other convertible words as well to make sentences.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Another rule for these convertible words is that words in English that end A-N-T or E-N-T, most of them we can just stick an E on the end and they become Spanish.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So for example, important becomes importante. Where is the stress there?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: In the second to last syllable. Good. The stress is as much part of the word as the sounds, which is why we want to get into the habit of just observing where the accent is, not trying to memorize it.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it is important'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it is not important'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it is not different'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'it is constant'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: If you want to say 'constantly', you can say constantemente. It is one word: constantemente. How would you say constantly?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So how would you say legally?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you think you say real in Spanish?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Real. Good. When we have an R at the beginning, you might hear it rolling a bit.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How do you think you would say really?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Realmente. Good. Real also means royal. Real Madrid is not 'real' Madrid, it's 'Royal' Madrid.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: So what would it mean if I said to you: normalmente es importante?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Normally, as in usually, it's important. Very good. You can use normalmente like 'usually'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Now, if we have a J-sound in a convertible word, this J is going to become a soft sound in Spanish, like the 'ch' in the Scottish 'loch'. Think of Argentina - the G in Argentina has this sound.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Global. Good. This G is not a J-sound, so it stays the same.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say general? Remember the J-sound becomes that soft throat sound.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: General. Very good. We're causing a bit of friction in the throat.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'naturally, it's original'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Naturalmente, es original. Good. You can also use naturalmente a bit like 'obviously'.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Words ending I-B-L-E or A-B-L-E, we can also convert many of them. We just pronounce it syllable by syllable and put the accent second from the end.

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: How would you say 'probably it's original'?

turns.speaker: tutor
turns.text: Flexible. Good. These rules give us over 3,000 words - enough to be very conversational. Feel free to experiment with the vocabulary!
